summary: Gruber examines his site's server logs from Safari's first week of public beta to see which browsers his readers actually use. Despite widespread complaints that Safari is worthless without tabbed browsing, it has already become the most popular browser among Daring Fireball readers. The two most popular Mac OS X browsers are platform-exclusive (Safari and Chimera), while the factory-default IE for Mac has fallen to third place. The post uses real data to gently rebuke the conventional wisdom about tabs being a dealbreaker, while offering wry observations about browser market share among his tech-savvy readership.
key_points: Safari became the #1 browser among DF readers in its first week as a public beta, despite lacking tabbed browsing
The two most popular Mac OS X browsers (Safari and Chimera) are platform-exclusive, pushing the factory-default IE Mac to third place
A significant number of readers visit from Windows, prompting Gruber's quip to 'get back to work'
Niche browsers like iCab outperformed Netscape 6/7 on Mac, and Opera on Mac had a negligible four users
Gruber acknowledges his readership is not a representative sample of Mac users but finds the trends telling nonetheless
money_quotes: Give me tabs or give me death.
In its first week of existence, as a public beta with numerous shortcomings, Safari has already become the number one web browser for Daring Fireball readers.
The two most popular browsers for Daring Fireball readers on Mac OS X are unique to OS X, and the factory default browser is in third place.
A lot of you are reading Daring Fireball from Windows boxes. Get back to work.
More of you are using iCab than Netscape 6/7 (on the Mac, at least). Would you have believed this to be possible three years ago?
Only 4 readers using Opera for Macintosh. Ouch.
key_insight: Real-world adoption data from Safari's first week showed that users were flocking to it despite its lack of tabbed browsing, undermining the loud consensus that tabs were a prerequisite for a viable browser.
